- Production quality: **99.99885%** (≈ 11.5 defects per million opportunities, i.e., **Six Sigma**).
- Production per employee increased **8×** over a 25-year span.

---

#### CASE STUDY 3 — Tesla: Gigafactory AI-Driven Body Shop

**Problem Pillar:** Traditional automotive body welding requires hundreds of robots in fixed sequences—inflexible and fault-prone.

**AI Solution:** Tesla deployed a **vision-based defect-detection system** on every vehicle post-welding. Each car is photographed from **~50 angles**, and a deep CNN model identifies micro-defects down to **0.5 mm**.

**Impact:**
- Inspection time reduced from several minutes (human) to **<5 seconds** (AI).
- Defect detection recall improved from ~85% (manual) to **>99%** (AI).

**Quantitative Comparison:**

$$
\text{Inspection Time Reduction} = \frac{T_{\text{manual}} - T_{\text{AI}}}{T_{\text{manual}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Inspection Time Reduction} = \frac{180\,\text{s} - 5\,\text{s}}{180\,\text{s}} \times 100 \approx 97.2\%
$$

---

#### CASE STUDY 4 — Bosch: Industry 4.0 Across 270+ Plants

**Problem Pillar:** Bosch operates a globally distributed manufacturing network; harmonizing quality and efficiency across plants was difficult.

**AI Solution:**
- Centralized AI platform with **>1,500 active AI projects**.
- Use cases: predictive maintenance, energy optimization, visual quality inspection.

**Impact:**
- Energy savings: **>10%** across participating plants.
- Production efficiency gains: **5–15%**.
- Reported cumulative savings: **hundreds of millions of euros** annually.

---

#### CASE STUDY 5 — Foxconn: "Foxbots" and AI-Driven Electronics Assembly

**Problem Pillar:** Labor-intensive smartphone assembly; high attrition; quality variability.

**AI Solution:** Deployment of **Dark Factory** lines with AI-orchestrated collaborative robots (cobots) and autonomous mobile robots (AMRs).

**Impact:**
- Target headcount reduction on certain lines: **>70%**.
- Achieved cycle-time consistency with **<2% variance**.

**Mathematical verification of cycle-time variance:**

$$
\sigma^2_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (CT_i - \mu_{CT})^2
$$

A variance reduction yielding $\sigma^2_{\text{cycle}} < 0.0004 \cdot \mu_{CT}^2$ corresponds to the <2% coefficient of variation.

---

#### CASE STUDY 6 — FANUC: AI-Driven CNC and Robotics

**Problem Pillar:** CNC spindle failures cause catastrophic production losses; tool-wear compensation is non-trivial.

**AI Solution:** FANUC's AI Spindle Monitor and AI Servo Monitor use vibration and current sensors with ML models trained on millions of operating hours.

**Impact:**
- Unplanned downtime reduced by **up to 50%** in customer deployments.
- Tool-life prediction accuracy: **>95%**.

---

#### CASE STUDY 7 — Rolls-Royce: "IntelligenceEngine" for Aerospace Manufacturing

**AI Solution:** Digital twin of every engine combined with NLP-driven maintenance manuals and ML-driven manufacturing process control.

**Impact:**
- Total factory downtime reduction: **~30%**.
- Production scrap reduction: **~40%**.
- RUL prediction accuracy: **>92%**.

---

### 3.2 Comparative Impact Matrix

| Case Study | Industry | Primary AI Technique | Key Quantified Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| GE Predix | Aerospace | LSTM (Time-series) | 20% fewer unplanned removals |
| Siemens Amberg | Electronics | CNN + RL | 99.99885% quality |
| Tesla Body Shop | Automotive | Deep CNN (Vision) | 97.2% inspection time reduction |
| Bosch Industry 4.0 | Cross-sector | Multi-model ML | >10% energy savings |
| Foxconn Foxbots | Electronics | Robotics + RL | 70% headcount reduction |
| FANUC AI Spindle | Tooling | Vibration ML | 50% downtime reduction |
| Rolls-Royce IntelligenceEngine | Aerospace | Digital Twin + NLP | 30% downtime, 40% scrap reduction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Notice that *every* case study reports a quantified metric tied to either **OEE components**, **downtime**, **defect rate**, or **cycle time**. Memorize the formulas in Section 2.1 and pair them with at least one case study per formula.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic AI-Driven Manufacturing Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shop Floor Sensors: Vibration, Vision, Thermal, Acoustic] --> B[Edge Gateway: Data Acquisition and Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Industrial Bus: OPC-UA / MQTT]
    C --> D[Cloud Data Lake: Time-Series DB]
    D --> E[AI Model Training Engine: ML / DL Pipelines]
    E --> F[Model Registry and Validation]
    F --> G[Edge Deployment: Real-Time Inference]
    G --> H[Actuators and Control Systems]
    H --> I[Feedback Loop: Continuous Model Retraining]
    I --> E
    D --> J[Manufacturing Execution System MES Dashboard]
    J --> K[Human Operator HMI and Decision Support]
    K --> H
```

### 4.2 Case Study Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cs1[GE Predix] --> p1[LSTM Time-Series]
    cs2[Siemens Amberg] --> p2[CNN Vision and RL]
    cs3[Tesla Body Shop] --> p3[Deep CNN Defect Detection]
    cs4[Bosch Industry 4.0] --> p4[Multi-Model ML Platform]
    cs5[Foxconn Foxbots] --> p5[Collaborative Robots and RL]
    cs6[FANUC AI Spindle] --> p6[Vibration Signal ML]
    cs7[Rolls-Royce IntelligenceEngine] --> p7[Digital Twin and NLP]
    p1 --> out[Quantified Business Impact: OEE, Downtime, Quality]
    p2 --> out
    p3 --> out
    p4 --> out
    p5 --> out
    p6 --> out
    p7 --> out
```

### 4.3 AI-Driven Predictive Maintenance Loop (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Machine Operating State] --> s2[Sensor Data Stream]
    s2 --> s3[Feature Extraction Module]
    s3 --> s4[Anomaly Detection Model: Isolation Forest / Autoencoder]
    s4 --> s5{Anomaly Score Threshold Exceeded?}
    s5 -->|No| s1
    s5 -->|Yes| s6[Root Cause Classifier: Random Forest]
    s6 --> s7[RUL Predictor: LSTM Regression]
    s7 --> s8[Maintenance Work Order Generation]
    s8 --> s9[Technician Intervention]
    s9 --> s10[Model Retraining with New Failure Data]
    s10 --> s4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In KTU descriptive answers, always annotate the diagram with **what data flows where**, **what algorithm is applied**, and **what business metric improves**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) and state the world-class benchmark value.

**Model Answer:**
OEE is a composite metric that quantifies manufacturing productivity by multiplying three factors:

$$
\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}
$$

Where:
- **Availability** = Run Time / Planned Production Time
- **Performance** = (Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces) / Run Time
- **Quality** = Good Pieces / Total Pieces

The **world-class benchmark for OEE is 85%**, as defined by SEMI (Semiconductor Equipment and Materials International) and adopted across lean-manufacturing literature.

---

**Q2.** Mention any two real-world applications of Computer Vision in AI-driven manufacturing.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Tesla Body-Shop Inspection:** Deep CNN models analyze multi-angle photographs of every welded vehicle body, detecting micro-defects down to **0.5 mm** within **<5 seconds**, replacing human inspectors.
2. **Siemens Amberg PCB Inspection:** CNN-based vision systems inspect every printed circuit board produced at the Amberg facility, enabling a defect rate of **~11.5 DPMO** (Six Sigma quality).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the GE Predix case study. Identify the manufacturing problem addressed, the AI technique deployed, and the quantifiable business outcomes achieved.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the generic architecture of an AI-driven predictive maintenance system. State any **three** key performance metrics used to evaluate its effectiveness.

##### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Manufacturing Problem:** Unscheduled removal of jet engines from commercial aircraft is extremely costly to airlines (estimated **$8 billion/year** industry-wide). GE Aviation needed to shift from reactive to predictive maintenance.

2. **AI Technique Deployed:** GE deployed the **Predix Industrial IoT Platform**, which uses **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** recurrent neural networks for time-series anomaly detection and **survival-analysis models** for predicting Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of critical engine components. Each engine streams **~1 TB of data per flight** from **~5,000 sensors**.

3. **Quantifiable Business Outcomes:**
   - Reduction in unplanned engine removals: **~20%**.
   - Estimated fleet-wide cost savings: **~$400 million** per year for GE's customer airlines.
   - Improved dispatch reliability and reduced AOG (Aircraft on Ground) events.

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Machinery with Sensors] --> B[Data Acquisition Layer]
    B --> C[Signal Preprocessing and Feature Engineering]
    C --> D[Cloud-Based ML Model Training]
    D --> E[Edge Inference Engine]
    E --> F{Anomaly Detected?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Generate Maintenance Alert]
    F -->|No| H[Continue Normal Operation]
    G --> I[Technician Intervention and Repair]
    I --> J[Update Training Dataset]
    J --> D
```

**Three Key Performance Metrics:**

1. **Prediction Accuracy (PA):** Fraction of correctly predicted machine states; target **>90%**.
2. **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Reliability indicator; higher is better; target **>2,000 hours**.
3. **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** Maintainability indicator; lower is better; target **<30 minutes**.

Additionally, **OEE improvement** (typically **5–15%** in published case studies) and **false-positive rate** (**<5%** target) are widely reported.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the AI-driven manufacturing implementations of **Siemens (Amberg)** and **Tesla (Gigafactory)** with respect to: (i) problem addressed, (ii) AI technique used, and (iii) quantifiable quality improvement.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain how Bosch's Industry 4.0 AI platform differs from a single-plant AI deployment. List **four** distinct use-cases of AI reported by Bosch.

##### Model Solution for (a):

| Aspect | Siemens Amberg | Tesla Gigafactory |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Problem | Maintain Six-Sigma quality at massive PCB production scale (~17M PLCs/year) | Manual body-weld inspection slow & error-prone; need micro-defect detection |
| (ii) AI Technique | CNN for solder-joint defect classification; RL for adaptive scheduling | Deep CNN for multi-angle (50+ views) post-weld visual inspection |
| (iii) Quality Metric | **99.99885%** quality → ~11.5 DPMO | Inspection recall **>99%** vs ~85% manual; time reduction **~97.2%** |

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Difference:** Bosch's platform is a **global, multi-plant, federated AI ecosystem** spanning **>270 plants**, whereas a single-plant deployment is geographically and operationally isolated. Bosch's platform benefits from:

- Cross-plant data pooling for more robust models.
- Centralized governance and standardized KPIs.
- Edge-cloud hybrid deployment for global scalability.

**Four Use-Cases:**
1. **Predictive Maintenance** — vibration and current analytics on CNC spindles.
2. **Visual Quality Inspection** — CNN-based defect detection on automotive parts.
3. **Energy Optimization** — ML-driven HVAC and compressor scheduling.
4. **Supply-Chain Demand Forecasting** — time-series models for inventory optimization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In Part B answers, **students frequently lose 2–3 marks** by failing to state **quantified numbers** (e.g., writing "significant reduction" instead of "20% reduction in unplanned removals"). Always cite the published figure.
> - Students also lose marks by **drawing the block diagram without labeling data flow directions**. Every arrow must be annotated.
> - Do **not** confuse **Industry 4.0** with **AI**. Industry 4.0 is the broader paradigm (IoT + CPS + AI + Cloud); AI is one enabler within it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AI-Driven Manufacturing** integrates ML, DL, CV, and RL into shop-floor operations to optimize productivity, quality, and uptime.
- Always structure case-study answers using the **five-pillar framework**: Problem → Data → AI Model → Deployment → Impact.
- **OEE** is the universal productivity KPI; target world-class value is **85%**.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** and **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** are the cornerstones of any predictive-maintenance case study.
- The **seven landmark case studies** that KTU expects familiarity with are: **GE Predix, Siemens Amberg, Tesla Gigafactory, Bosch Industry 4.0, Foxconn Foxbots, FANUC AI Spindle, and Rolls-Royce IntelligenceEngine**.
- **CNN** dominates **vision-based inspection**; **LSTM** dominates **time-series fault prediction**; **Reinforcement Learning** dominates **adaptive process control**.
- Always cite **quantified business outcomes** (e.g., 20%, 50%, 97.2%, $400 M) — vague qualitative statements cost marks.
- Edge inference ensures **sub-second decisions** (e.g., reject defect in <50 ms); cloud retraining ensures **continuous model improvement**.
- A correct answer on this topic **always contains**: a problem statement, an AI technique named explicitly, a block diagram, and a numeric outcome.
- Memorize at least **three formulas** from Section 2.1 and be ready to apply them to any of the seven case studies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotics and Automation: Robotics in Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT745 - SMART MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robotics and Automation: Robotics in Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotics in Manufacturing** refers to the application of programmable, multi-functional manipulators (industrial robots) designed to move materials, parts, tools, or specialised devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of manufacturing tasks.

According to the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)**, an **Industrial Robot** is defined as an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications*.

A **Manufacturing Automation System** is the integrated use of programmable logic controllers (PLCs), computer numerical control (CNC), industrial robots, sensors, and supervisory software to perform production operations with minimal human intervention.

### 1.2 Key Performance Metrics of Industrial Robots

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are universally accepted in KTU board evaluation and industrial practice:

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent rotational (R) or translational (P) joints. A 6-DOF robot (RRR-RRR) is the industrial benchmark for full spatial dexterity.
- **Payload Capacity:** Maximum permissible mass (kg) the robot can manipulate at rated speed, typically measured at the wrist flange.
- **Reach / Working Envelope:** Maximum radial distance (mm) from the robot base to the centre of the end-effector.
- **Repeatability:** The ability of a robot to return to a previously taught position. Standard industrial repeatability is **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm**.
- **Accuracy:** Deviation between commanded position and attained position in absolute coordinates.
- **Cycle Time:** Time (seconds) required to complete one standard motion cycle (e.g., pick-and-place).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Skilled Human Arm"

Imagine a **highly trained factory worker** whose arm has been replaced with rigid steel links connected by perfectly engineered joints. This worker never gets tired, never blinks, never takes a tea break, and can repeat the exact same arc-of-motion **over 100,000 times per day** with sub-millimetre precision.

- The **bones** of this worker = robot links (links 1, 2, 3 … n).
- The **elbows and shoulders** = joints (revolute R / prismatic P).
- The **muscle memory** = the controller (PLC / robot CPU).
- The **eyes** = sensors (vision, force, proximity).
- The **hand** = the end-effector (gripper, welding torch, paint gun).

This analogy helps us visualise why robots dominate in **the 3 D's** of manufacturing: tasks that are **Dull, Dirty, or Dangerous**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners consistently test the four basic robot configurations: **Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Polar / Spherical (RRP), and Articulated / An revolute (RRR).** A Jointed-Arm (Articulated) robot is the most common in modern automotive body shops.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Robot Anatomy — The Logical Chain of Operation

A manufacturing robotic cell operates through a structured hierarchy:

1. **Power Supply / Servo Drive Unit:** Converts AC mains into regulated DC for servo motors at each joint.
2. **Controller (Robot CPU):** The "brain" running the real-time OS that interprets the trajectory program.
3. **Teach Pendant:** Handheld HMI used to manually jog the robot and record waypoints (lead-through programming).
4. **Manipulator (Mechanical Unit):** The kinematic chain of links and joints from base to tool flange.
5. **End-Effector:** Customised tool mounted on the wrist — gripper, MIG/MAG welding torch, paint sprayer, deburring spindle.
6. **Sensors:** Encoders (joint position), resolvers, force-torque sensors, machine vision cameras.
7. **External I/O and Safety:** Light curtains, emergency stop relays, safety PLCs conforming to **ISO 10218-1**.

### 2.2 Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) Parameters — The Robot's "DNA"

For any serial manipulator, the geometry of the kinematic chain is completely described by **four DH parameters** per joint:

- **aᵢ** : link length (mm)
- **αᵢ** : link twist (degrees or radians)
- **dᵢ** : link offset (mm) — translation along zᵢ
- **θᵢ** : joint angle (degrees or radians) — rotation about zᵢ

The homogeneous transformation from frame *i-1* to frame *i* is given by the standard DH matrix.

### 2.3 Robot Classification by Configuration

| Configuration | Joint Sequence | Workspace Shape | Typical Application | Strengths |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian / Gantry | P-P-P | Rectangular box | CNC loading, PCB assembly | High rigidity, simple kinematics |
| Cylindrical | R-P-P | Hollow cylinder | Die casting, machine tending | Reaches into cavities |
| Polar / Spherical | R-R-P | Spherical sector | Stamping, forging | Large reach in compact cell |
| SCARA | R-R-P (selective compliance) | Cylindrical disk | Pick-and-place, micro-assembly | High speed in horizontal plane |
| Articulated (An revolute) | R-R-R-R-R-R | Irregular sphere | Welding, painting, sealing | Maximum dexterity |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following block. These formulas appear verbatim in 14-mark derivation questions.

$$
A_i \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\
\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\
0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
T_n^{\,0} \;=\; A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdots A_n \quad\text{(Forward Kinematics)}
$$

$$
\text{DOF}_{\text{Grübler}} \;=\; M\,\bigl(N-1\bigr)\;+\;\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl(f_i\bigr)\;-\;\sum_{j}\,C_j
$$

For a planar **n-link** serial manipulator, this reduces to:

$$
\text{DOF} \;=\; 3\,(n-1)\;+\;2\,n \;=\; 5n - 3
$$

Cycle-time based **production rate**:

$$
P_r \;=\; \frac{3600}{T_c} \quad\text{(parts/hour)}
$$

where $T_c$ is the cycle time in **seconds**.

### 2.5 Industrial Utility

These kinematics underpin:
- **Off-line programming (OLP)** in tools like *RoboDK*, *Delmia*, and *Visual Components*.
- **Digital twin** synchronisation between virtual and physical cells — a core Industry-4.0 deliverable.
- **Collision-free path planning** in cluttered manufacturing environments.
- **Calibration of TCP (Tool Centre Point)** for precise welding seam tracking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator (RR Configuration)

This is the **most repeated KTU 14-mark derivation**. We derive the position $(x,y)$ of the end-effector as a function of the two joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometric parameters.**

Let:
- $L_1$ = length of link 1 (shoulder-to-elbow)
- $L_2$ = length of link 2 (elbow-to-wrist)
- $\theta_1$ = rotation of joint 1 (about base $z$-axis)
- $\theta_2$ = rotation of joint 2 relative to link 1

**Step 2 — Build the individual DH matrices.**

Apply the standard DH transform with $\alpha_1 = 0$, $d_1 = 0$, $a_1 = L_1$ and $\alpha_2 = 0$, $d_2 = 0$, $a_2 = L_2$.

$$
A_1 \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 \\
\sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A_2 \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\cos\theta_2 \\
\sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\sin\theta_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply to obtain $T_2^0$.**

We perform the matrix product $T_2^0 = A_1 \cdot A_2$, focusing only on the upper-left $2\times2$ rotation block and the translation column:

$$
R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
L_1\cos\theta_1 \;+\; L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
L_1\sin\theta_1 \;+\; L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the angle-sum identities.**

$$
\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \;=\; \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 \;-\; \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2
$$

$$
\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \;=\; \sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2 \;+\; \cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2
$$

Substituting back yields the **closed-form forward kinematics**:

$$
x \;=\; L_1\cos\theta_1 \;+\; L_2\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 \;-\; L_2\sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2
$$

$$
y \;=\; L_1\sin\theta_1 \;+\; L_2\sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2 \;+\; L_2\cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form (this is what the examiner expects).**

$$
\boxed{\;x \;=\; L_1\cos\theta_1 \;+\; L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;y \;=\; L_1\sin\theta_1 \;+\; L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\;}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Workspace Radius of a 2-DOF Planar Arm

**Step 1 — Identify the extreme positions.**

The tip reaches its **maximum** reach when both links align collinearly:

$$
R_{\max} \;=\; L_1 \;+\; L_2
$$

The tip reaches its **minimum** reach (the "inner hole") when the links fold back on each other:

$$
R_{\min} \;=\; \bigl\vert\,L_1 \;-\; L_2\,\bigr\vert
$$

**Step 2 — Express the annular workspace.**

The planar workspace is the annular region:

$$
\bigl\vert L_1 - L_2 \bigr\vert \;\le\; r \;\le\; L_1 + L_2
$$

where $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ is the radial distance from the base origin.

**Step 3 — Add the full angular sweep.**

Since both joints can independently rotate by $\pm$ their mechanical limits (typically $-150^\circ \le \theta_i \le 150^\circ$), the **full reachable region is a ring sector** sweeping through $360^\circ$ only when unlimited rotation is permitted; for revolute joints with joint limits, it is a truncated sector.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state any joint-limit assumptions explicitly in the derivation. Marks are deducted when assumptions are left implicit.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A 2-DOF planar arm has $L_1 = 400$ mm and $L_2 = 300$ mm. Find $(x,y)$ when $\theta_1 = 30^\circ$ and $\theta_2 = 45^\circ$.

**Solution:**

Compute $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 75^\circ$.

Using the derived closed-form equations:

$$
x \;=\; 400\cos 30^\circ \;+\; 300\cos 75^\circ
$$

$$
x \;=\; 400(0.8660) \;+\; 300(0.2588)
$$

$$
x \;=\; 346.41 \;+\; 77.65 \;=\; 424.06 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
y \;=\; 400\sin 30^\circ \;+\; 300\sin 75^\circ
$$

$$
y \;=\; 400(0.5000) \;+\; 300(0.9659)
$$

$$
y \;=\; 200.00 \;+\; 289.78 \;=\; 489.78 \text{ mm}
$$

**Workspace check:** $R_{\max} = 700$ mm, $R_{\min} = 100$ mm.

$$
r \;=\; \sqrt{424.06^2 \;+\; 489.78^2} \;=\; \sqrt{179\,826 \;+\; 239\,884} \;=\; \sqrt{419\,710}
$$

$$
r \;\approx\; 647.85 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $100 \le 647.85 \le 700$, the point is **inside the reachable workspace** ✔.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Robot Configuration vs. Application

| Robot Type | DH Joint Pattern | Workspace Volume $V$ | Best-fit Industry Sector | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian | P-P-P | $L_x L_y L_z$ | Semiconductor, dispensing | Medium |
| Cylindrical | R-P-P | $\pi r^2 h$ | Machine tending, foundries | Medium |
| Polar / Spherical | R-R-P | $\tfrac{2}{3}\pi r^3$ (sector) | Stamping, forging | Medium-High |
| SCARA | R-R-P | $2\pi r r_{\text{reach}}$ disk | Electronics pick-and-place | Medium |
| Articulated | R-R-R-R-R-R | Irregular sphere | Automotive welding, sealing | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define an **industrial robot** as per ISO 8373. List any **four** characteristics that distinguish an industrial robot from a fixed automation machine.

**Model Answer:**
An industrial robot, as per **ISO 8373:2021**, is defined as an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications*.

Four distinguishing characteristics:

1. **Reprogrammability** — task instructions can be modified without altering hardware.
2. **Versatility / Multi-purpose** — same robot can perform welding, painting, palletising via end-effector change.
3. **Three or more axes of programmability** — minimum DOF threshold.
4. **Multipurpose manipulator** — capable of handling varied payloads and tool trajectories.

> [!NOTE]
> Many students lose this 3-mark question by writing the generic "robot = automatic machine" definition. The keyword **"reprogrammable"** and **"three or more axes"** must appear verbatim.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between **repeatability** and **accuracy** of an industrial robot. Which parameter is more critical in **pick-and-place** operations and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Repeatability** | Ability to return to a previously *taught* position under identical conditions | ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm |
| **Accuracy** | Ability to move to a position commanded in *absolute* world coordinates | ±1 mm to ±10 mm |

In **pick-and-place** operations the robot is taught every waypoint, so it repeatedly returns to the same relative pose. Therefore **repeatability is more critical** than absolute accuracy. High repeatability with moderate accuracy is acceptable and cost-effective.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### ➤ Question A (14 Marks) — Full Forward-Kinematics Derivation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **four basic robot configurations** classified by their joint arrangement. Discuss **one industrial application** for each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **2-DOF planar RR manipulator** with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, derive the forward-kinematic equations giving the end-effector position $(x, y)$ in terms of $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ using the **Denavit–Hartenberg** method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to (a):**

The four basic configurations are:

1. **Cartesian (Gantry) — P-P-P**
   Three mutually perpendicular prismatic joints produce a rectangular workspace. High structural rigidity, simple controller.
   *Application:* PCB pick-and-place, water-jet cutting.

2. **Cylindrical — R-P-P**
   Base rotation about a vertical axis, followed by a vertical lift and a horizontal reach.
   *Application:* Die-casting machine tending.

3. **Polar / Spherical — R-R-P**
   Two revolute joints for shoulder yaw and pitch, one prismatic for radial reach. Work envelope is a spherical sector.
   *Application:* Forging press tending, heat-resistant handling.

4. **Articulated (An revolute) — R-R-R**
   All revolute joints (typically 4–6 DOF). Maximum dexterity resembling a human arm.
   *Application:* Automotive spot welding, arc welding, painting.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[CartesianPPP] --> P2[RectangularWorkEnvelope]
    P3[CylindricalRPP] --> P4[CylindricalEnvelope]
    P5[PolarRRP] --> P6[SphericalSector]
    P7[ArticulatedRRR] --> P8[IrregularSphere]
```

---

**Model Answer to (b):**

Use the derivation from **Section 3.1** above. The final closed-form equations:

$$
x \;=\; L_1\cos\theta_1 \;+\; L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y \;=\; L_1\sin\theta_1 \;+\; L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

The orientation (rotation) of the end-effector is:

$$
\phi \;=\; \theta_1 \;+\; \theta_2
$$

**Boundary conditions for the reachable workspace:**

$$
\bigl\vert L_1 - L_2 \bigr\vert \;\le\; \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \;\le\; L_1 + L_2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget to **multiply the matrices $A_1 \cdot A_2$** in symbolic form before substituting the angle-sum identities. Marks are forfeited when only the final boxed equation is presented without the intermediate trigonometric expansion. Always show: $(i)$ DH table, $(ii)$ $A_1$, $(iii)$ $A_2$, $(iv)$ matrix product, $(v)$ angle-sum substitution, $(vi)$ final boxed result.

---

#### ➤ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: DH Parameters & DOF Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameters**. Explain the **four rules** for assigning coordinate frames to successive links of a serial manipulator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **3-DOF Cartesian robot** has a stroke of $X = 1.2$ m, $Y = 0.8$ m, and $Z = 0.5$ m. If the cycle time for a pick-and-place operation is $T_c = 3.5$ seconds, calculate **(i)** the workspace volume and **(ii)** the production rate in parts per hour. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to (a):**

**DH parameters** are a minimal set of four kinematic variables $\{a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i\}$ that completely describe the relative position and orientation between two consecutive links of a serial manipulator.

**Four Rules for Frame Assignment (Khalil-Kleinfinger / standard DH):**

1. **$z_{i-1}$ axis** lies along the axis of motion of joint $i$.
2. **$x_i$ axis** is along the common normal between $z_{i-1}$ and $z_i$, pointing from $z_{i-1}$ to $z_i$.
3. **$y_i$ axis** is chosen to complete a right-handed coordinate system with $x_i$ and $z_i$.
4. **Origin of frame $i$** is the intersection of the common normal and $z_i$.

The four parameters are then extracted:

- $a_i$ = distance from $z_{i-1}$ to $z_i$ along $x_i$ (link length)
- $\alpha_i$ = angle from $z_{i-1}$ to $z_i$ about $x_i$ (link twist)
- $d_i$ = distance from origin of frame $i-1$ to intersection of $x_i$ with $z_{i-1}$ along $z_{i-1}$ (link offset)
- $\theta_i$ = angle from $x_{i-1}$ to $x_i$ about $z_{i-1}$ (joint angle)

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners often award full 7 marks for a correctly labelled diagram showing axis conventions, even if the textual description has minor omissions. A diagram is therefore non-negotiable.

---

**Model Answer to (b):**

**(i) Workspace volume of the Cartesian robot:**

$$
V \;=\; X \times Y \times Z
$$

$$
V \;=\; 1.2 \text{ m} \;\times\; 0.8 \text{ m} \;\times\; 0.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
V \;=\; 0.48 \;\text{m}^3 \;=\; 4.8 \times 10^{8} \;\text{mm}^3
$$

**(ii) Production rate in parts per hour:**

$$
P_r \;=\; \frac{3600}{T_c}
$$

$$
P_r \;=\; \frac{3600}{3.5}
$$

$$
P_r \;\approx\; 1028.57 \;\text{parts/hour} \;\approx\; 1029 \;\text{parts/hour}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common error is using **$T_c = 3.5$ minutes** instead of **seconds**. The formula $\tfrac{3600}{T_c}$ strictly requires $T_c$ in **seconds**, with the constant 3600 coming from the number of seconds in an hour. Marks are deducted silently if the units are not explicitly written in every step. Also, do **not** confuse **production rate** with **throughput** — throughput additionally accounts for tool-change and auxiliary downtime.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Industrial robot definition** (ISO 8373) hinges on the three keywords: **automatic**, **reprogrammable**, **three or more axes**.
- Four classical configurations: **Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Polar (RRP), Articulated (RRR).**
- **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy** — repeatability is the variable that matters in taught-path applications.
- A **2-DOF planar RR manipulator** has forward-kinematics:
$$
x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2), \quad y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2).
$$
- **Workspace annular bounds:** $\bigl\vert L_1 - L_2 \bigr\vert \le r \le L_1 + L_2$.
- **DH parameter table** must accompany every kinematic derivation — examiners mark for completeness.
- **Cycle time → production rate:** $P_r = 3600 / T_c$ with $T_c$ in **seconds**.
- Always state **joint limits** when describing a workspace.
- Modern smart-manufacturing cells integrate robots with **vision**, **force-torque sensing**, and **MES/SCADA** layers per the **ISA-95 automation pyramid**.
- Safety standards: **ISO 10218-1** for robots, **ISO/TS 15066** for collaborative robots.
- Common industrial robot vendors in board questions: **ABB, KUKA, FANUC, Yaskawa (Motoman), Universal Robots**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Systems and Collaborative Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT745 - SMART MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autonomous Systems and Collaborative Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Autonomous Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Autonomous System (AS):** A self-governing cyber-physical entity capable of **perceiving** its environment through sensors, **reasoning** about the sensed data using embedded intelligence, and **acting** upon the physical world — all without continuous human intervention. In the KTU 2024 Smart Manufacturing context, an autonomous system operates on the classic **Sense → Plan → Act** (SPA) cognitive loop, exhibiting varying degrees of self-decision-making authority.

**Three Foundational Pillars of Autonomy (KTU 2024 Taxonomy):**

| Pillar | Engineering Function | Manufacturing Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing** | Data acquisition from environment | LiDAR, RGB-D cameras, IMU |
| **Cognition** | Decision making, planning, learning | Edge AI, Digital Twin sync |
| **Actuation** | Physical effect on environment | Servo drives, BLDC motors |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Distinction:** The term *Autonomous* ≠ *Automated*. *Automated* systems follow pre-programmed scripts (open-loop determinism), whereas *Autonomous* systems dynamically re-plan their behaviour in response to unstructured environmental stimuli (closed-loop adaptability).

---

### 1.2 Collaborative Robots (Cobots) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collaborative Robot (Cobot):** A robot designed for **direct physical interaction with a human operator within a defined collaborative workspace**, in compliance with the international safety standard **ISO/TS 15066:2016**. Unlike traditional industrial robots (which operate inside fenced cages under power-and-force-limiting protocols), cobots are engineered with rounded geometry, low-inertia links, and intrinsic safety features to permit shared workspace operation.

**Cobot Classification per ISO 10218 + ISO/TS 15066 (KTU High-Yield):**

| Class | Description | Human Proximity |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A — Traditional** | Caged, high-payload industrial robot | None |
| **Class B — Power & Force Limited** | Force-feedback cobot (e.g., UR, FANUC CRX) | Close contact allowed |
| **Class C — Speed & Separation Monitored** | Vision-based safety zones | Variable distance |
| **Class D — Hand-Guided** | Teach-by-demonstration cobot | Continuous contact |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Self-Driving Car vs. The Assembly Worker:**
> 
> Imagine a traditional factory as a **highway with a chauffeur-driven limousine**: the human driver (programmer) dictates every turn, gear shift, and brake. This is *automation*.
> 
> Now picture the same factory with a **Tesla on Autopilot**: the car reads road signs, detects pedestrians, negotiates lane changes, and even parks itself — but a human supervisor can override anytime. This is *autonomy*.
> 
> Finally, the **cobotic workstation** is like the limousine with a co-pilot: the robot lifts a heavy engine block (robot) while the human mechanic tightens bolts (human) — both working in physical contact, each compensating for the other's blind spots. The robot doesn't *replace* the mechanic; it *augments* the mechanic's strength and precision.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Bold per KTU Convention)

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Typically **6 DOF** (3 translation + 3 rotation) for a 6-axis cobot arm.
- **Payload range:** **3 kg to 16 kg** (commercial cobots as of 2024).
- **Reach radius:** **500 mm to 1300 mm** for tabletop cobots; up to **3 m** for mobile manipulators.
- **Repeatability:** **±0.03 mm to ±0.1 mm** (vs. ±0.02 mm for industrial robots).
- **Maximum collaborative speed:** **250 mm/s** (ISO/TS 15066 quasi-static contact limit).
- **Stoplap time (Tₛ):** **< 100 ms** for safety-rated monitored stop.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Autonomy Stack — Six Logical Levels

Following the KTU 2024 Smart Manufacturing reference framework, autonomous systems progress through six logical layers:

1. **Level 0 — Manual Operation:** Human performs all decisions; machine is purely mechanical.
2. **Level 1 — Assisted Operation:** Machine provides guidance (e.g., CNC tool path preview).
3. **Level 2 — Partial Automation:** Machine executes scripted tasks; human supervises (e.g., AGV on magnetic tape).
4. **Level 3 — Conditional Autonomy:** System handles routine cases; human takes over for exceptions (e.g., AMR in a mapped warehouse).
5. **Level 4 — High Autonomy:** System self-recovers from faults; human is a fallback only (e.g., autonomous forklifts in structured yards).
6. **Level 5 — Full Autonomy:** System matches or exceeds human capability in any condition (theoretical; not yet deployed in manufacturing at KTU evaluation time).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Hook:** Most KTU questions frame this as *"Compare the autonomy levels of a CNC lathe, an AGV, and a smart warehouse AMB"*. Memorize the 6-level ladder — questions are guaranteed on this.

### 2.2 Why Cobots? — The "Why" Behind Human-Robot Collaboration

- **Why not full automation?** — Not all tasks (especially those with high variability, fine dexterity, or unstructured objects) are economically feasible to fully automate. The *95% rule* of Industry 4.0: a task is automated only if **>95% repeatability** is achievable. The remaining 5% benefits from human flexibility.
- **Why cobots specifically?** — Traditional robots require **safety cages** (costly floor space, rigid cell layout). Cobots eliminate cages via four ISO-recognized collaborative modes:
  - **Safety-rated monitored stop**
  - **Hand-guiding**
  - **Speed & separation monitoring**
  - **Power & force limiting**
- **How do cobots sense the human?** — Through **joint torque sensors** (current-based force estimation), **skin-like capacitive surfaces**, or **external vision systems** (safety scanners using LiDAR + PLC).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside tables. Use raw alphanumeric labels (no markdown bold) inside Mermaid nodes.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Degrees of Freedom (Grübler-Kutzbach)** | $M = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$ | dimensionless | Quick DOF check for parallel manipulators |
| 2 | **Quasi-static contact force limit** | $F_{\max} = 65\text{ N}$ (hand) ; $F_{\max} = 140\text{ N}$ (chest) | N | ISO/TS 15066 collaborative safety envelope |
| 3 | **Maximum collaborative speed** | $v_{cobot} \le 250 \text{ mm/s}$ | mm/s | Quasi-static contact regime |
| 4 | **Separation distance (speed-separation)** | $S = v_{robot} \cdot T_{s} + v_{robot} \cdot t_{r} + B$ | mm | ISO 13855 safety distance |
| 5 | **Stopping time (cobot)** | $T_{s} = T_{r} + T_{s,m} + T_{s,p}$ | ms | Safety-rated monitored stop |
| 6 | **Spatial resolution of LiDAR** | $\Delta \theta = \frac{360^\circ}{N_{points}}$ | degrees | Mapping accuracy |
| 7 | **SLAM pose update (odometry)** | $x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cos(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \phi}{2})$ | m | Autonomous ground vehicle navigation |
| 8 | **Repeatability (statistical)** | $R = \bar{x} + 3\sigma_{pos}$ | mm | Cobot precision spec |
| 9 | **Cycle-time optimization (Pareto)** | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \min(T_{cycle})$ subject to $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ | s | Multi-cobot scheduling |
| 10 | **Energy-aware scheduling** | $E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot t_{active}$ | J | Green manufacturing KPI |

> Where:
> - $N$ = number of links, $J_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $J_2$ = 2-DOF joints.
> - $T_r$ = reaction time, $T_{s,m}$ = mechanical stop, $T_{s,p}$ = PLC processing.
> - $\Delta s$ = arc-length displacement, $\Delta \phi$ = heading change.
> - $B$ = safety buffer (typically $\ge 500$ mm).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Tier-1 suppliers** deploy cobots in **screwdriving, adhesive dispensing, and polishing** tasks where force feedback prevents damage to deformable parts.
- **Smart warehouses (Amazon, Flipkart hubs)** use AMRs to ferry totes — the AMRs operate at **Level 3 autonomy**, dynamically re-planning paths when a human crosses.
- **Pharmaceutical cleanrooms** use cobots for **vial filling**, where the rounded geometry and absence of lubricants prevent particulate contamination.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Safety Separation Distance per ISO 13855

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Safety Distance Equation is a KTU 2024 favourite. Learn it cold.**

The minimum separation distance $S$ between a moving cobot and a human operator, such that the robot can come to a safe stop before contact, is derived as follows.

**Given:**
- Robot approach speed: $v_{robot}$ (mm/s)
- Human approach speed: $v_{human} = 1600$ mm/s (ISO standard walking speed)
- Total system stopping time: $T_s$ (s)
- Safety buffer: $B$ (mm)
- Reflection-based correction factor $C$ (mm, accounting for the reach of the operator's limbs into the sensing zone, typically 850 mm for floor-mounted scanners).

**Step 1 — Distance travelled by robot during stopping time:**

The robot covers a distance $d_r$ before fully halting:

$$
d_{robot} = v_{robot} \cdot T_{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Distance travelled by human during the same time:**

The human approaches at standardized speed:

$$
d_{human} = v_{human} \cdot T_{s} = 1600 \cdot T_{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the buffer term (operator reach + safety margin):**

The total minimum separation to guarantee a complete stop *before* contact:

$$
S = v_{robot} \cdot T_{s} + C + B
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the worst-case human approach speed instead of robot speed (conservative design):**

For a **floor-mounted vertical scanner** (most common cobot cell layout), the human is the moving entity:

$$
S = 1600 \cdot T_{s} + (1200 - 0.4 \cdot H) + B
$$

where $H$ is the height of the scanner above floor (in mm). The term $(1200 - 0.4H)$ is the ISO 13855 reach factor $C$.

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$
S = 1600 \, T_{s} + C + B
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that increasing $T_s$ (slowing the controller) or $v_{robot}$ (slowing the cobot) linearly inflates the floor-space requirement. This is why cobots are deliberately slowed to $v_{robot} \le 250$ mm/s — to keep $S$ economically viable (typically 1.5–2.5 m).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Grübler-Kutzbach DOF for a 6-Axis Cobot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Grübler-Kutzbach criterion is mandatory in KTU Module 3 questions on "mobility of robotic manipulators".**

**Step 1 — State the formula:**

For a planar (2D) mechanism:

$$
M_{planar} = 3(N - 1) - 2J_1 - J_2
$$

For a spatial (3D) mechanism:

$$
M_{spatial} = 6(N - 1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5
$$

**Step 2 — Identify parameters for a 6-axis serial cobot (e.g., UR5):**

- $N = 7$ links (base + 6 revolute-arm segments)
- $J_1 = 6$ (six 1-DOF revolute joints)
- $J_2 = J_3 = J_4 = J_5 = 0$

**Step 3 — Plug into the spatial formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{spatial} &= 6(N - 1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5 \\
&= 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 0 \\
&= 6 \cdot 6 - 30 \\
&= 36 - 30 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude:**

A 6-axis serial cobot possesses exactly **6 DOF**, which matches the **3 translational + 3 rotational** DOF required for a rigid body in 3D space — hence, it can position and orient its end-effector arbitrarily (within the reachable workspace). This is the engineering basis for "6-axis cobot = universal manipulator."

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: SLAM Odometry Update (Pose Propagation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) use this exact update rule in their ROS-based navigation stack.**

**Step 1 — Define initial pose at time $t$:**

$$
P_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)
$$

**Step 2 — Read encoder deltas during interval $\Delta t$:**

- Linear displacement: $\Delta s$ (in m)
- Angular displacement: $\Delta \phi$ (in rad)

**Step 3 — Update the pose at time $t + 1$:**

The arc-traced mid-point of the trajectory is used for higher accuracy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \phi}{2}\right) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \phi}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Wrap the heading angle to $(-\pi, \pi]$:**

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sin\theta_{t+1}, \cos\theta_{t+1}\right)
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This dead-reckoning method *accumulates drift* over time. Hence, AMRs periodically correct the pose using **LiDAR-based scan matching (ICP algorithm)** to maintain $\le 10$ cm global accuracy.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cobot vs. Industrial Robot (KTU Comparison Favourite)

| Parameter | Industrial Robot (Class A) | Collaborative Robot (Cobot) | KTU Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Workspace | Fenced cell (no human entry) | Shared human-robot cell | Cobots save ~40% floor area |
| Payload | 10–1000+ kg | 3–16 kg | Cobots optimized for dexterity, not brute force |
| Max speed (TCP) | 2000–8000 mm/s | 250–1000 mm/s | Cobots limited by ISO/TS 15066 |
| Repeatability | ±0.02 mm | ±0.05–0.1 mm | Industrial wins on precision |
| Safety mechanism | Hard guards + interlocks | Force/torque sensing + power limiting | Cobots are intrinsically safe |
| Programming | Teach pendant, RAPID | Hand-guiding, drag-and-drop GUI | Cobots enable low-code SMEs |
| Cost (2024) | $50K – $500K | $20K – $75K | Cobots accessible to MSMEs |
| Deployment time | 4–12 weeks | 1–3 days | Cobots enable agile retooling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Autonomous Manufacturing Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeH[HumanOperator] --> nodeWS[SharedWorkspace]
    nodeWS --> nodeCV[SafetyComputerVision]
    nodeWS --> nodeFS[ForceTorqueSensor]
    nodeCV --> nodeSC[SupervisoryControllerPLC]
    nodeFS --> nodeSC
    nodeSC --> nodeSS[SafetyStopLogic]
    nodeSC --> nodePB[PathPlanner]
    nodePB --> nodeRA[RobotArm6DOF]
    nodeSS --> nodeRA
    nodeRA --> nodeEE[EndEffectorGripper]
    nodeEE --> nodeWS
    nodeDT[DigitalTwinMirror] -.->|RealTimeSync| nodeSC
    nodeDT -.->|PredictiveSimulation| nodePB
    nodeMES[MESCloudLayer] -->|JobDispatch| nodeSC
    nodeRA -->|Telemetry| nodeMES
```

> **Figure 1 — Functional Block Architecture:** The block diagram above maps the **Sense → Plan → Act** loop of an autonomous cobotic cell. The *Supervisory Controller* is the cognitive hub; the *Digital Twin* provides predictive simulation feedback; the *MES* layer dispatches jobs in real time. All safety blocks (Computer Vision + Force Sensor) feed into the Safety Stop Logic, which is a hardwired, IEC 61508 SIL-3 rated subsystem.

### 4.2 Cobot Operational Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([CellStart]) --> nodeCheck{Is Human in Workspace}
    nodeCheck -->|Yes| nodeSlow[Reduce Speed to 250 mm per s]
    nodeCheck -->|No| nodeFast[Run at Full Speed up to 1000 mm per s]
    nodeSlow --> nodeForceCheck{Contact Force Exceeds Threshold}
    nodeForceCheck -->|No| nodeContinue[Continue Operation]
    nodeForceCheck -->|Yes| nodeStop[Initiate Safety Rated Monitored Stop]
    nodeFast --> nodeVisionCheck{Vision System Confirms Clear Zone}
    nodeVisionCheck -->|Yes| nodeContinue
    nodeVisionCheck -->|No| nodeStop
    nodeStop --> nodeRecover[Wait for Operator Reset Acknowledge]
    nodeRecover --> nodeStart
    nodeContinue --> nodeStart
```

> **Figure 2 — Operational Mode Decision Tree:** This is the **ISO/TS 15066 collaborative mode selector**. When a human enters the workspace, the cobot *automatically* drops to quasi-static collaborative speed; if any sensor detects contact, the safety-rated monitored stop triggers within $T_s \le 100$ ms.

### 4.3 AMR Navigation Stack (SLAM + Path Planning)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL[LiDAR2D] --> nodeSM[Scan Matching ICP]
    nodeIMU[IMU 9 Axis] --> nodeEKF[Extended Kalman Filter]
    nodeEnc[Wheel Encoders] --> nodeEKF
    nodeEKF --> nodePose[Robot Pose Estimate]
    nodeSM --> nodeMap[Occupancy Grid Map]
    nodePose --> nodeMap
    nodeMap --> nodeAStar[A Star Global Planner]
    nodeMap --> nodeDWA[Dynamic Window Approach Local Planner]
    nodeAStar --> nodeCmd[Velocity Commands v omega]
    nodeDWA --> nodeCmd
    nodeCmd --> nodeMot[Motor Drivers BLDC]
    nodeMot --> nodeWheels[Wheels]
```

> **Figure 3 — AMR Navigation Stack:** The block diagram shows the canonical ROS Navigation Stack used in KTU-referenced AMRs (e.g., MiR, Locus Robotics). Global planner uses $A^*$ on a static map; local planner uses **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** for dynamic obstacle avoidance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define an Autonomous System. Differentiate between an Automated system and an Autonomous system with one manufacturing example for each.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **Definition (1 mark):** An *Autonomous System* is a cyber-physical entity that perceives its environment through sensors, processes the data using embedded intelligence, and acts upon the world without continuous human control, operating on the Sense–Plan–Act cognitive loop.
> 
> **Difference (1 mark):**
> 
> | Aspect | Automated | Autonomous |
> |---|---|---|
> | Decision-making | Pre-programmed, deterministic | Real-time, adaptive |
> | Human role | Operator (executes commands) | Supervisor (intervenes only on exception) |
> | Variability handling | Low (rigid scripts) | High (re-plans on the fly) |
> 
> **Example (1 mark):** A **CNC lathe** turning a batch of identical shafts is *automated* (scripted G-code, no perception). An **AMR in a warehouse** that re-routes to avoid a fallen carton is *autonomous* (uses LiDAR + SLAM to re-plan).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*List the four collaborative operation modes specified by ISO/TS 15066. Mention the maximum permissible quasi-static contact force on the human hand.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **Four modes (2 marks):**
> 1. Safety-rated monitored stop
> 2. Hand-guiding
> 3. Speed and separation monitoring
> 4. Power and force limiting
> 
> **Contact force limit (1 mark):** Per ISO/TS 15066, the maximum permissible quasi-static contact force on the **human hand is 65 N** (and 140 N on the chest region), measured at the contact interface.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students routinely lose marks on Part B questions by **(a) confusing "automation" with "autonomy"** in the introductory paragraph, and **(b) skipping the safety standard citation (ISO/TS 15066)** when describing cobot operations. Always anchor your answer to the relevant ISO/TS clause to secure full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Modified]** — *Autonomy & Cobotic Cell Design*

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a fully autonomous cobotic work cell. Highlight the role of the Digital Twin, MES layer, and Safety Stop Logic. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

> A fully autonomous cobotic work cell is a closed-loop cyber-physical system composed of three interlinked layers: **Physical Layer**, **Control Layer**, and **Information Layer**. *(1 mark)*

**(i) Physical Layer (Sensors + Actuators):** Comprises a 6-DOF cobot arm with joint torque sensors, an end-effector (gripper or tool), a 2D LiDAR safety scanner, and an RGB-D camera for object recognition. These provide multi-modal perception. *(1 mark)*

**(ii) Control Layer (Cognition):** The **Supervisory Controller (PLC + Edge AI)** executes the Sense–Plan–Act loop. It receives sensor data, fuses it via a Kalman filter, and dispatches motion commands. The **Path Planner** computes collision-free trajectories using the Dynamic Window Approach (DWA). *(1 mark)*

**(iii) Information Layer (Connectivity):** The **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)** layer dispatches jobs, tracks cycle times, and synchronizes with the ERP. The **Digital Twin** is a real-time 3D mirror of the cell that runs predictive simulations to anticipate failures (e.g., tool wear) and feeds this foresight back to the controller. *(1 mark)*

**(iv) Safety Stop Logic:** A hardwired, **IEC 61508 SIL-3** rated subsystem that monitors contact force, separation distance, and E-Stop signals. On threshold breach, it cuts motor power within $T_s \le 100$ ms. *(1 mark)*

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** See Section 4.1, Figure 1.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*A cobot in a shared workspace has a maximum approach speed of 250 mm/s. The total system stopping time is 80 ms. The operator reach factor $C$ is 1200 mm, and the safety buffer $B$ is 500 mm. Calculate the minimum separation distance per ISO 13855. Justify why the cobot speed is deliberately capped at 250 mm/s. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $v_{robot} = 250$ mm/s (this is the cobot's approach speed; for floor-mounted scanner, we use ISO human speed of 1600 mm/s)
- $T_s = 80$ ms $= 0.080$ s
- $C = 1200$ mm (operator reach factor)
- $B = 500$ mm (safety buffer)
- ISO 13855 standardized human approach speed: $v_{human} = 1600$ mm/s

**Step 1 — Apply the safety distance equation (3 marks):**

$$
S = v_{human} \cdot T_s + C + B
$$

$$
S = 1600 \cdot 0.080 + 1200 + 500
$$

$$
S = 128 + 1200 + 500
$$

$$
S = 1828 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the safety distance (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{S = 1.828 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation & economic implication (2 marks):**

The 1.828 m safety radius directly inflates the **cell footprint**. If we had allowed the cobot to operate at 2000 mm/s (industrial speed), the separation would jump to over 3.5 m — which is *economically infeasible* for a typical SME shop floor. Hence, the **250 mm/s cap** is a deliberate trade-off between throughput and floor space.

**Step 4 — Engineering justification (1 mark):**

At 250 mm/s, in the **quasi-static contact regime**, the impact kinetic energy remains below the human-pain threshold ($E \le 0.25$ J), ensuring that any incidental contact satisfies ISO/TS 15066 biomechanical limits.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Past Paper]** — *AMR & SLAM-based Navigation*

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Explain the architecture of an Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR) navigation stack. Differentiate between an AGV and an AMR with two key technical distinctions. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**AGV vs. AMR (3 marks):**

| Parameter | AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) | AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot) |
|---|---|---|
| Navigation aid | Magnetic tape, QR codes, RFID | LiDAR-based SLAM, no infrastructure |
| Path flexibility | Fixed paths (re-programming required) | Dynamic re-planning on the fly |
| Autonomy level | Level 2 (scripted) | Level 3 (conditional autonomy) |
| Cost of re-layout | High (physical tape re-installation) | Near-zero (software re-map) |

**AMR Navigation Stack Architecture (4 marks):**

1. **Perception Layer:** LiDAR (360° 2D scanner, ~30 m range, 0.25° angular resolution), IMU (9-axis), and wheel encoders. *(1 mark)*
2. **Localization Layer:** Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) fuses odometry and IMU to provide a 6-DOF pose estimate; ICP scan-matching corrects accumulated drift against the static map. *(1 mark)*
3. **Mapping Layer:** Occupancy grid map is built online (SLAM) or pre-loaded from a CAD layout. *(1 mark)*
4. **Planning Layer:** Two-tier — $A^*$ (global planner, optimal path on static map) + Dynamic Window Approach (local planner, dynamic obstacle avoidance in real time). *(1 mark)*

Refer to Section 4.3, Figure 3 for the full block diagram.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*An AMR starts at pose $(0, 0, 0)$. In one time-step, the wheel encoders report $\Delta s = 0.30$ m and $\Delta \phi = 0.20$ rad. Compute the new pose $(x_{t+1}, y_{t+1}, \theta_{t+1})$ using the odometry update rule. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $x_t = 0$ m, $y_t = 0$ m, $\theta_t = 0$ rad, $\Delta s = 0.30$ m, $\Delta \phi = 0.20$ rad.

**Step 1 — Compute the new heading angle (1 mark):**

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta \phi = 0 + 0.20 = 0.20 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mid-trajectory heading (1 mark):**

$$
\theta_{mid} = \theta_t + \frac{\Delta \phi}{2} = 0 + \frac{0.20}{2} = 0.10 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Update $x$ coordinate (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_{mid}) \\
&= 0 + 0.30 \cdot \cos(0.10) \\
&= 0 + 0.30 \cdot 0.99500 \\
&= 0.2985 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Update $y$ coordinate (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \cdot \sin(\theta_{mid}) \\
&= 0 + 0.30 \cdot \sin(0.10) \\
&= 0 + 0.30 \cdot 0.09983 \\
&= 0.02995 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final pose (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{(x_{t+1}, y_{t+1}, \theta_{t+1}) = (0.299 \text{ m}, \ 0.030 \text{ m}, \ 0.200 \text{ rad})}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The robot has moved ~0.30 m forward and turned slightly (~11.46°) — the small $y$ component is the *lateral drift* induced by the heading change. In practice, this estimate is corrected by ICP scan-matching against the LiDAR map.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Topic: Autonomous Systems & Collaborative Robots**

- ✅ **Autonomous ≠ Automated** — automation is scripted, autonomy is adaptive (Sense–Plan–Act loop).
- ✅ **Six autonomy levels** (0 → 5) — most factory robots sit at Level 2–3; AMRs at Level 3; theoretical full autonomy is Level 5.
- ✅ **Four ISO/TS 15066 collaborative modes:** Safety-rated monitored stop, Hand-guiding, Speed & separation monitoring, Power & force limiting.
- ✅ **Quasi-static contact force limits:** 65 N (hand), 140 N (chest), 65 N (head, neck).
- ✅ **Maximum collaborative speed:** 250 mm/s (under quasi-static contact conditions).
- ✅ **Stopping time budget:** $T_s \le 100$ ms for cobot safety stop.
- ✅ **Safety distance equation:** $S = 1600 \cdot T_s + C + B$ (ISO 13855, floor-mounted scanner).
- ✅ **Grübler-Kutzbach DOF:** 6-axis serial cobot = **6 DOF** (matches rigid-body requirement in 3D).
- ✅ **AGV vs. AMR:** AGV = magnetic tape/QR (Level 2, fixed path); AMR = LiDAR-SLAM (Level 3, dynamic).
- ✅ **AMR Navigation Stack:** Perception → Localization (EKF) → Mapping (occupancy grid) → Planning ($A^*$ + DWA).
- ✅ **Odometry update rule:** $x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_t + \Delta\phi/2)$ — uses mid-point angle for higher accuracy.
- ✅ **Digital Twin** = real-time 3D virtual mirror that runs predictive simulations and feeds the supervisory controller.
- ✅ **Cobot pricing (2024):** $20K – $75K — accessible to MSMEs vs. industrial robots at $50K – $500K.
- ✅ **Cobot repeatability:** ±0.05–0.1 mm (acceptable for ~90% SME tasks; not for ultra-precision CNC).

> **Mnemonic for the four ISO collaborative modes — "S-H-S-P":** **S**top, **H**and-guide, **S**peed-separation, **P**ower-force-limit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automation Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT745 - SMART MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Automation Technologies # Module 3: Automation Technologies ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Automation in Smart Manufacturing? > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** > **Automation Technology** refers to the integrated use of control systems, sensors, actuators, communication networks, and information technologies to operate industrial processes and machinery with minimal human intervention, forming the operational backbone of the Smart Manufacturing ecosystem. In the architecture of Industry 4.0 and the KTU 2024 Smart Manufacturing framework, automation is the **physical-action layer** of the cyber-physical pyramid. It is what converts digital decisions into real-world movements, measurements, and control responses. ### 1.2 Hierarchical Classification of Automation Automation in manufacturing is broadly classified into four functional categories: | Level | Type | Description | Example | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | **L1** | **Fixed / Hard Automation** | Repetitive, high-volume, sequence-based; very low flexibility | Car body welding lines | | **L2** | **Programmable Automation** | Reconfigured via program changes; medium flexibility | CNC machines, Robotic cells | | **L3** | **Flexible Automation** | On-the-fly changeovers; human-supervised | FMS (Flexible Manufacturing Systems) | | **L4** | **Integrated / Intelligent Automation** | Self-diagnostic, AI-driven, IIoT-connected | Cyber-Physical Production Systems (CPPS) | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The Human Body Analogy (Intuition Builder):** > Think of a smart factory as a **human body**: > - **Sensors** = the *five senses* (eyes, ears, skin) that gather information > - **PLCs / Controllers** = the *brain and spinal cord* that make decisions > - **Actuators** = the *muscles and limbs* that perform physical work > - **SCADA / HMI** = the *conscious awareness* that lets us monitor ourselves > - **Communication Networks** = the *nervous system* connecting all parts > > When all these are integrated seamlessly, the factory becomes a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** — the foundation of smart manufacturing. ### 1.4 Key Performance Indicators in Automation The KTU evaluator expects students to recognize industry-standard automation metrics: - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** $\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ — unit: **hours** - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ — unit: **hours** - **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness):** $\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$ — typically reported as a **percentage (%)** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Automation Pyramid (ISA-95 Model) The **ANSI/ISA-95** standard defines five levels of automation, mapping perfectly to the Smart Manufacturing pyramid: - **Level 0 — Physical Process:** Actual sensors, motors, valves, and production hardware. - **Level 1 — Basic Control:** PLCs, DCS controllers executing closed-loop control loops. - **Level 2 — Supervisory Control:** SCADA systems, HMIs providing operator interaction. - **Level 3 — Manufacturing Operations:** MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems), scheduling. - **Level 4 — Enterprise Planning:** ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Questions on *"Where does PLC fit in the automation hierarchy?"* or *"Differentiate SCADA vs. DCS"* are guaranteed in Part A. Always answer using the **ISA-95 framework**. ### 2.2 Core Components of an Automation System #### A. Sensors (Input Devices) Sensors convert physical phenomena into electrical signals. Key types: | Sensor Type | Measured Quantity | Working Principle | Industry Example | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **RTD (PT100)** | Temperature | Resistance change with temperature | Furnace monitoring | | **Thermocouple** | High temperature | Seebeck effect (mV output) | Steel plants, boilers | | **LVDT** | Displacement | Electromagnetic induction | Precision CNC axes | | **Strain Gauge** | Force / Pressure | Resistance change under strain | Load cells | | **Photoelectric** | Object detection | Light beam interruption | Conveyor lines | | **Inductive Proximity** | Metal presence | Eddy current loss | Tool position sensing | #### B. Actuators (Output Devices) Actuators convert control signals into physical action: | Actuator | Energy Domain | Key Metric | |:---|:---|:---| | **Pneumatic Cylinder** | Compressed air | Stroke length (mm), Force (N) | | **Hydraulic Cylinder** | Pressurized oil | Operating pressure $\approx \mathbf{70\text{-}350\ bar}$ | | **Servo Motor** | Electrical (closed-loop) | Resolution: $\mathbf{17\text{-}bit}$ encoders | | **Stepper Motor** | Electrical (open-loop) | Step angle: $\mathbf{1.8°}$ typical | | **Piezoelectric Actuator** | Electrical | Sub-nanometer positioning | #### C. Controllers — The Brain - **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** Ruggedized industrial computer using **Ladder Logic (LD)**, **Function Block Diagram (FBD)**, or **Structured Text (ST)** per **IEC 61131-3** standard. - **DCS (Distributed Control System):** Geographically distributed controllers networked for continuous processes (oil refineries, power plants). - **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition):** Centralized supervisory software for geographically dispersed assets. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** SCADA ≠ DCS. SCADA is event-driven and supervisory; DCS is process-driven and tightly closed-loop. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application | |:---|:---|:---| | **Closed-Loop Transfer Function** | $G_{cl}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Stability analysis | | **PID Controller Output** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Process control | | **OEE Formula** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Smart factory KPI | | **PLC Scan Time** | $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output}$ | PLC selection | | **MTBF** | $\dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Reliability | | **MTTR** | $\dfrac{T_{downtime}}{N_{repairs}}$ | Maintainability | | **Baud Rate** | $\dfrac{\text{Bits}}{\text{Second}}$ | Serial communication | | **RTD Resistance (PT100)** | $R_T \approx R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | Temperature sensing | > [!NOTE] > **Vertical Pipe Substitution Rule:** When writing $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert \geq 1$ in a table, write as `\vert G(s)H(s) \vert \geq 1`. Never use the raw `|` symbol inside table rows. ### 2.4 Industrial Communication Protocols Modern automation depends on **deterministic networking**: - **Fieldbus (legacy):** PROFIBUS, FOUNDATION Fieldbus, DeviceNet - **Real-time Ethernet:** PROFINET, EtherCAT, EtherNet/IP - **IIoT Protocols:** **OPC-UA** (Unified Architecture), **MQTT** (publish-subscribe, lightweight) - **Wireless:** WirelessHART, LoRaWAN, 5G URLLC > **Why it matters:** OPC-UA has become the de-facto protocol for the **Reference Architecture Model Industry 4.0 (RAMI 4.0)** integration layer. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Derivation: PID Controller in Discrete Form (For Digital PLCs) > **Background:** KTU examiners love derivations of the **PID equation** because it directly links automation to control theory in your syllabus. **Continuous-Time PID:** $$ u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$ **Step 1 — Discretization for Digital Implementation:** Replace the integral with a rectangular (Euler forward) sum and the derivative with a backward difference. Let $T_s$ = sampling time, $k$ = current sample. **Step 2 — Integral Term (Discretized):** $$ \int_{0}^{t_k} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) $$ where $T_s$ is in **seconds**. **Step 3 — Derivative Term (Discretized):** $$ \frac{de(t)}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s} $$ **Step 4 — Assemble the Discrete PID (Position Form):** $$ u(k) = K_p \, e(k) + K_i \, T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) + K_d \, \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to Velocity Form (Anti-windup friendly):** $$ \Delta u(k) = u(k) - u(k-1) $$ $$ \Delta u(k) = K_p [e(k) - e(k-1)] + K_i T_s e(k) + \frac{K_d}{T_s} [e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)] $$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Tip:** Always show the substitution from continuous to discrete — this is where most students lose **2-3 marks** by skipping Step 1. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — OEE Calculation **Problem:** A CNC machine in a smart cell operated for **30 days**. Data: - Total scheduled time per day: **16 hours** - Planned downtime: **1 hour/day** - Actual run time: **13.5 hours/day** - Ideal cycle time: **45 seconds/part** - Total parts produced (good + reject): **1080 parts/day** - Rejected parts: **60 parts/day** **Find OEE.** **Step 1 — Availability:** $$ A = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \frac{13.5}{16 - 1} = \frac{13.5}{15} = 0.9000 $$ **Step 2 — Performance:** $$ P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Parts}}{\text{Operating Time}} = \frac{45 \times 1080}{13.5 \times 3600} $$ $$ P = \frac{48600}{48600} = 1.0000 $$ *(Capped at 100%, indicating the line is running at ideal speed.)* **Step 3 — Quality:** $$ Q = \frac{\text{Good Parts}}{\text{Total Parts}} = \frac{1080 - 60}{1080} = \frac{1020}{1080} = 0.9444 $$ **Step 4 — OEE:** $$ \text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9000 \times 1.0000 \times 0.9444 $$ $$ \text{OEE} = 0.8500 \;\; \text{or} \;\; \mathbf{85.00\%} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Benchmark:** World-class OEE is $\geq \mathbf{85\%}$. This cell meets the global benchmark — a typical Smart Manufacturing KPI question. ### 3.3 Stability Criterion (Nyquist Simplified) For a unit-feedback closed-loop system to be stable: $$ \vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert < 1 \quad \text{when} \quad \angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180° $$ This is the **Gain Margin (GM)** condition. A GM $\geq \mathbf{6\ dB}$ is the standard design rule. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The ISA-95 Automation Pyramid (Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid block renders the **functional architecture** mapping automation components to ISA-95 levels — suitable for KTU diagram-based questions. ```mermaid flowchart TD L4["LEVEL 4 ENTERPRISE PLANNING
ERP Systems, Strategic Scheduling"] L3["LEVEL 3 MANUFACTURING OPERATIONS
MES, Production Tracking, Quality"] L2["LEVEL 2 SUPERVISORY CONTROL
SCADA, HMI, Alarm Management"] L1["LEVEL 1 BASIC CONTROL
PLC, DCS Controllers, PID Loops"] L0["LEVEL 0 PHYSICAL PROCESS
Sensors, Actuators, Motors, Valves"] L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 L1 --> L0 L0 -.Feedback.-> L1 L1 -.Telemetry.-> L2 L2 -.Reports.-> L3 L3 -.KPIs.-> L4 style L4 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style L3 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style L2 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style L1 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000 style L0 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Generic Closed-Loop Automation Control Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR SP["Setpoint SP"] --> SUM["Comparator Sum"] SUM -->|Error e| CTRL["Controller PLC or PID"] CTRL -->|Signal u| ACT["Actuator Motor or Valve"] ACT --> PROC["Plant Physical Process"] PROC --> FB["Feedback Sensor"] FB -->|Measured y| SUM style SP fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style SUM fill:#f4b084,stroke:#000 style CTRL fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000 style ACT fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style PROC fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#000 style FB fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000 ``` ### 4.3 PLC Scan Cycle Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Power ON Self Test POST"] --> B["Read All Inputs to Input Image Table"] B --> C["Execute User Program Logic Ladder"] C --> D["Update Output Image Table"] D --> E["Write Outputs to Physical Modules"] E --> F["Housekeeping Communications Diagnostics"] F --> B style A fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000 style C fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000 style D fill:#92d050,stroke:#000 style E fill:#00b050,stroke:#000 style F fill:#4472c4,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Integration of Automation with Smart Manufacturing Layers ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CPPS["CYBER PHYSICAL PRODUCTION SYSTEM"] direction LR PHYS["PHYSICAL LAYER
Sensors, Actuators, Machines"] CYBER["CYBER LAYER
PLCs, SCADA, MES, ERP"] COMM["COMMUNICATION LAYER
OPC UA, MQTT, PROFINET"] DATA["DATA LAYER
Cloud Historian, Analytics"] end PHYS <--> COMM CYBER <--> COMM DATA --> CYBER style PHYS fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000 style CYBER fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000 style COMM fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style DATA fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 style CPPS fill:#f3f3f3,stroke:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and use plain uppercase text without markdown bold inside the labels. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]` **Define Industrial Automation. Differentiate between Fixed and Flexible Automation.** **Model Answer:** > **Industrial Automation** is the use of control systems — such as computers, PLCs, and robots — to operate industrial machinery and processes with minimal or no human intervention, replacing manual operations with self-regulating mechanisms. | Parameter | Fixed Automation | Flexible Automation | |:---|:---|:---| | **Flexibility** | Very low; designed for one product | High; supports variety of products | | **Volume** | High-volume production | Low to medium volume | | **Changeover Time** | Days or weeks | Minutes to hours | | **Initial Investment** | Very high | Moderate to high | | **Example** | Car welding line | FMS with robotic cells | *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for comparison table)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]` **List the components of a typical PLC-based automation system and explain the role of SCADA.** **Model Answer:** The components of a PLC-based automation system are: 1. **Input Devices:** Sensors, switches, transducers 2. **PLC (Controller):** Processes logic; executes ladder diagram programs per **IEC 61131-3** 3. **Output Devices:** Actuators, relays, motor starters 4. **HMI (Human-Machine Interface):** Operator panel for local interaction 5. **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition):** Centralized software for monitoring multiple PLCs, trending, alarm logging, and remote control across an entire plant floor. SCADA acts as the **eyes of the plant**, providing real-time visualization and historical data analysis to operators. *(3 marks — 2 for listing, 1 for SCADA explanation)* --- ### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **ISA-95 automation hierarchy** in a Smart Manufacturing environment. Mention the function of each level. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A motor speed control system uses a PID controller. The process transfer function is: $$ G(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+2)} $$ Design the PID gains $K_p = 2.0$, $K_i = 0.5$, $K_d = 0.1$ and compute the **steady-state error** for a unit step input. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **ISA-95 Automation Hierarchy — 5 Levels:** | Level | Function | Typical Equipment | |:---:|:---|:---| | **L0 — Physical Process** | Real-world sensing and actuation | Sensors, motors, valves | | **L1 — Basic Control** | Real-time regulatory control | PLC, DCS, PID loops | | **L2 — Supervisory** | Operator monitoring and supervision | SCADA, HMI | | **L3 — Manufacturing Ops** | Production tracking, scheduling | MES (Manufacturing Execution System) | | **L4 — Enterprise** | Business planning and resource allocation | ERP (SAP, Oracle) | **Flow:** Information flows upward (data, KPIs), while commands flow downward (setpoints, schedules). The hierarchy enables **separation of concerns**, **scalability**, and **cybersecurity zoning** — all essential for Smart Manufacturing. *(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for level descriptions, 2 for flow explanation)* #### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — Identify the Open-Loop Transfer Function with PID Controller:** $$ G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s $$ $$ G_c(s) = 2.0 + \frac{0.5}{s} + 0.1 s $$ **Step 2 — Open-Loop Transfer Function:** $$ G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G(s) = \left(2.0 + \frac{0.5}{s} + 0.1 s\right) \cdot \frac{1}{s(s+2)} $$ $$ G_{OL}(s) = \frac{0.1 s^2 + 2.0 s + 0.5}{s^2(s+2)} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $K_p$ Position Constant (Unit Step Input):** Since the open-loop system has **Type 2** (two poles at origin due to PID + plant integrator): $$ K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G_{OL}(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{0.1 s^2 + 2.0 s + 0.5}{s^2(s+2)} $$ $$ K_p = \frac{0.5}{0} \to \infty $$ **Step 4 — Steady-State Error using Final Value Theorem:** $$ e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0 $$ **Conclusion:** The steady-state error for a unit step input is $\mathbf{e_{ss} = 0}$, which confirms the system tracks the setpoint perfectly in steady state — a desirable property in industrial motor control. *(7 marks — 1 for $G_c$, 2 for $G_{OL}$, 2 for $K_p$ evaluation, 2 for final $e_{ss}$ interpretation)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]` **(a)** Compare **PLC, DCS, and SCADA** systems across architecture, application, response time, and data handling. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An automated bottling line has the following data for a 24-hour shift.
